title: allow to set loglevel for healthcheck
body:
### Feature request description

Currently our server logs are mostly full of healthcheck output, making them hard to navigate.

### Suggest potential solution

I would want the healthcheck service to run with `LogLevelMax=notice`, this would remove the normal output, inclusive the started/stopped messages from systemd itself.

I believe this should be the default, but there could be an option for `podman run` to override the loglevel for healtchecks.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

Filtering the log, but this is cumbersome.

### Additional context

_No response_

comments.body:
I have no problem with doing this by default, is there any use case where one might need that journal output? I would like to avoid adding a option for this to podman run. Maybe we could change it based on `--log-level` from podman.

@vrothberg @mheon WDYT?
I don't see a problem with this.
I agree these messages should be dropped down a level so they don't normally show up in the logs.
SGTM
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Please do this.  Thanks.  My logs are full of garbage because of this.

Are you talking specifically about this:
		logrus.Infof("Startup healthcheck for container %s passed, recreating timer", c.ID())
We could drop this to debug level.

> Are you talking specifically about this:
> logrus.Infof("Startup healthcheck for container %s passed, recreating timer", c.ID())

Nope, output from systemd and the healthcheck directly:

```
Apr 25 16:38:31 xserver systemd[1]: Started 88d1d5d1e8da165bd1afd8326dc27c36272be63d3e58ebe22c0f22f779cae5cd.service - /usr/bin/podman healthcheck run 88d1d5d1e8da165bd1afd8326dc27c3>
Apr 25 16:38:31 xserver podman[2383221]: 2023-04-25 16:38:31.476846721 +0200 CEST m=+0.028589918 container health_status 88d1d5d1e8da165bd1afd8326dc27c36272be63d3e58ebe22c0f22f779cae>
Apr 25 16:38:31 xserver podman[2383221]: 2023-04-25 16:38:31.560587797 +0200 CEST m=+0.112330971 container exec_died 88d1d5d1e8da165bd1afd8326dc27c36272be63d3e58ebe22c0f22f779cae5cd >
Apr 25 16:38:31 xserver systemd[1]: 88d1d5d1e8da165bd1afd8326dc27c36272be63d3e58ebe22c0f22f779cae5cd.service: Deactivated successfully.
Apr 25 16:39:32 xserver systemd[1]: Started 88d1d5d1e8da165bd1afd8326dc27c36272be63d3e58ebe22c0f22f779cae5cd.service - /usr/bin/podman healthcheck run 88d1d5d1e8da165bd1afd8326dc27c3>
Apr 25 16:39:32 xserver podman[2383282]: 2023-04-25 16:39:32.465572241 +0200 CEST m=+0.029527174 container health_status 88d1d5d1e8da165bd1afd8326dc27c36272be63d3e58ebe22c0f22f779cae>
Apr 25 16:39:32 xserver podman[2383282]: 2023-04-25 16:39:32.560574303 +0200 CEST m=+0.124529239 container exec_died 88d1d5d1e8da165bd1afd8326dc27c36272be63d3e58ebe22c0f22f779cae5cd >
Apr 25 16:39:32 xserver systemd[1]: 88d1d5d1e8da165bd1afd8326dc27c36272be63d3e58ebe22c0f22f779cae5cd.service: Deactivated successfully.
```

Changing that (transient) service to `LogLevelMax=notice` immediately stops this spam.
Should be that [systemd-run invocation](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/205cb5025000d8e7787689a0e815aade17f55f40/libpod/healthcheck_linux.go#L53).
(No clue why you additionally open up an DBUS Connection just to close it again)



> We could drop this to debug level.

You could, but this is not he culprit
The `exec_died` event can probably be removed (it's purely extraneous given this is a healthcheck), but we'll have to retain the `health_status` events for logging healthcheck runs.

> The `exec_died` event can probably be removed (it's purely extraneous given this is a healthcheck), but we'll have to retain the `health_status` events for logging healthcheck runs.

You are aware that this will produce 3600 log entries per hour, with the content "ok, nothing to see here"?

I would expect no output unless either:

-   the state changed between OK <-> NOK
-   the healthcheck failed

To get systemd to shutup about `Started` / `Deactivated` you have to run `systemd-run` with `-p LogLevelMax=5`, which will also hide the rest of the messages.
The only thing that should be logged are health check failures - be sure to log them with `warning` level.
Yes, we do realize this, but these messages (at least, the `health_status` logs) are an essential part of Podman's event logging system and cannot be removed.

> Yes, we do realize this, but these messages (at least, the `health_status` logs) are an essential part of Podman's event logging system and cannot be removed.

Well... realistically that means I will deactivate health checks, and roll my own if I find the time. it worked painless with docker =(

I think we can make this behavior configurable and suppress events emitted by health checks (but opt-in).

Alternatively, you can use `podman --events-backend=file`.  But the journald/systemd backend integrates much nicer into a modern Linux system.

> it worked painless with docker =(

@nolange can you elaborate on that?  Does Docker not emit the `exec` events etc. for health checks even when the events backend is set to journald?

I switched from Docker, so I cant easily test now but one machine still has some service running. No, there arent any log entries from health checks (json file) and I dont see why they should show up in logs (unless the checks fail)?
the state starting/healthy is still shown with `docker ps`

I would expect healthchecks to report the state directly to podman/docker and the container framework then deciding whether to log (ideally: only statechange) or stop/restart the container. I don't understand why the system log seems your internal means of communication, and the health state seemingly depending on it.

> I don't understand why the system log seems your internal means of communication, and the health state seemingly depending on it.

I don't think Podman depends on that, so we should fairly easily be able to tweak this behavior.

> Yes, we do realize this, but these messages (at least, the `health_status` logs) are an essential part of Podman's event logging system and cannot be removed.

@mheon can you elaborate why you think this cannot be removed?

These are effectively debug/audit messages that show admins that healthchecks are, in fact, running - it's an important part of debugging when things start to go wrong - the events show the status of the healthcheck, so when debugging you can check for intermittent failures in a monitored container via `podman events`. We could potentially add an opt-in for disabling HC events, but we absolutely cannot do something like that by default, it'd be very confusing to anyone trying to debug a healthcheck issue.

It looks like we can drop the log level of the systemd timers we create, though, which will eliminate the `systemd[1]` messages. That's a clear win for readability and we don't use those at all internally. Ideally we produce only 1 message per HC, the `health_status` event, which should reduce logging output by 75%.

> These are effectively debug/audit messages that show admins that healthchecks are, in fact, running - it's an important part of debugging when things start to go wrong - the events show the status of the healthcheck, so when debugging you can check for intermittent failures in a monitored container via `podman events`.

I wouldn't find errors in the sea of noise currently. If healthchecks are failing or not executed then **that** should be detected somehow.

> We could potentially add an opt-in for disabling HC events, but we absolutely cannot do something like that by default, it'd be very confusing to anyone trying to debug a healthcheck issue.

I don't think debugging healthcheck issues should be the default, I am not sure if some container user should even know they exist. For container author of course there should be options for debugging logs.

We'd also love to get rid of:
```
Sep 25 13:52:50 XXX systemd[1]: Started /usr/bin/podman healthcheck run fd61ed0a66e2f8737abbafe5acf223fd0c3c35e135d8876042db6456681fbaa0.
Sep 25 13:52:50 XXX systemd[1]: fd61ed0a66e2f8737abbafe5acf223fd0c3c35e135d8876042db6456681fbaa0.service: Succeeded.
```
this really clutters our logs :/

Could this be considered for backporting to 4.7? (Assuming another release will be cut for 4.7 at all)

EDIT:// Preferably I would have it in RHEL 8/9 which is at 4.4 currently, but I guess that is a bit far back.

Sure, although I am not sure we will be cutting another release, 4.8 will be cut in November.

/cherry-pick 4.7
running LTS systems means we will have to live with this all the lifetime of EL9 ??? (or disabling the healthcheck altogether)
9.4 is getting Podman 4.9 which will have this fix

> running LTS systems means we will have to live with this all the lifetime of EL9 ??? (or disabling the healthcheck altogether)

You can also reach out through the customer channels of your distribution of choice and ask for backports if you desire it before 9.4.
Note this fix is causing a regression (https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/20342), so no backports until the regression is fixed.
